title: Co-Creator of Haskell: Functional Programming, Thinking in Types, Useless Languages | Simon Peyton Jones
guest_role: Co-creator of Haskell, GHC lead; now designing Verse at Epic Games

sections.title: Why Haskell is talked about more than used
Should students continue to learn programming
Why Excel is his 2nd favorite programming language

key_takeaways: Functional programming is 'programming with values instead of mutation' — mathematics made executable; Church's lambda calculus and Turing's machine are provably equivalent in power, and Simon's research life has been one experiment: what happens if you exclude side effects by default?

FP has been the mainstream's idea laboratory: garbage collection, lambdas, language-integrated query, parametric polymorphism, and static typing all grew up in functional languages before being adopted by imperative ones.

Building hardware for functional languages (Lisp machines, MIT's Monsoon dataflow machine, the SKIM SK-combinator machine) was an 'inspiring mistake': it built interpreters in hardware, doing at runtime what a compiler does better at compile time, and could never compete with Intel's investment in x86 — even redesigning the ISA for FP would add almost nothing.

C's unchecked mutation makes the Internet 'a boat built out of paperclips': writing infrastructure in Haskell or ML would remove roughly 99% of exploits (buffer overruns, pointer bugs) by construction, though no language prevents high-level flaws like deadlock; Rust keeps C's usefulness while moving far toward safety.

Haskell started very safe but useless (version 1 had no I/O — a program was just a string-to-string function) and climbed toward usefulness without abandoning purity; laziness forced Haskell to stay pure and led to monads, while OCaml's strict evaluation order allowed impure I/O for free — he calls the two languages siblings that learn from each other.

Laziness is modularity glue (John Hughes's 'Why Functional Programming Matters'): you can generate an infinite chess game tree in one module and prune/explore it in a completely separate one, where strictness forces you to fuse them; monads then make effects first-class typed values, with purity preserved because dirtiness is declared in types like IO Int, refined further by effect systems such as Tom Ellis's Bluefin.

The biggest benefit of static types is maintainability and design: GHC is a 35-year-old Haskell codebase he refactors fearlessly by changing a type and following the wave of compile errors; weak type systems are strictly worse than strong ones, and dynamic typing should be an explicit escape hatch for the rare programs static types reject.

GHC's architecture: parse, rename, typecheck, then desugar the ~50-node-type Haskell AST into Core — a tiny System F lambda calculus with about eight constructors that has barely changed in 35 years — then core-to-core optimization passes, C-- portable assembly, and native or LLVM backends; Core is fully type-checkable, which he claims no other production compiler's intermediate language is, catching optimizer bugs that would otherwise be distant segfaults.

'Avoid success at all costs' is a deliberate double parse: don't seek success by compromising the core no-unrestricted-side-effects principle, and don't get so successful you can't change anything — though today he spends weeks on backward-compatibility work GHC never used to promise.

LLMs 'may be the best thing that's happened to statically typed languages for a long time' because the compiler prunes the generation space in a tight iteration loop; but copilots need pilots — he won't merge unreviewed AI-generated code into GHC, students should still learn to program, and every child should know 'it's all just bits': ChatGPT is a trillion floating-point numbers plus arithmetic.

His day job at Epic Games is Verse, a functional logic language exploring a region of design space as far beyond functional languages as FP was beyond imperative in 1980 — the same weird-but-worth-it bet, now with a games company's resources; and Excel is the world's most widely used programming language by three orders of magnitude, whose pure formula language became Turing complete when his 20-year push added LAMBDA.

Advice to his younger self: all the successful people are making it up as they go along, so it's fine if you are too — and if you want to be lucky, put yourself in positions where lucky accidents can happen, which means taking risks.

notable_quotes.speaker: Simon Peyton Jones
notable_quotes.quote: When the limestone of imperative programming has worn away, the granite of functional programming will be revealed underneath.

notable_quotes.speaker: Simon Peyton Jones
notable_quotes.quote: It's like we've built a boat out of paperclips and we're surprised that it's leaky... we wrote our computational infrastructure for the world in an insecure language.

notable_quotes.speaker: Simon Peyton Jones
notable_quotes.quote: I think it may be the best thing that's happened to statically typed languages for a long time... with a static type system, you cut down the space of programs that the LLM can generate.

notable_quotes.speaker: Simon Peyton Jones
notable_quotes.quote: If you want to be lucky, you need to put yourself in positions where lucky things could happen. And that means taking some kind of risk.
